stimuli.id: Congruent Stroop stimulus (word matches ink color)

detection.selector: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus .stroop-stim'); if (!el) return false; const trial = window.jsPsych && window.jsPsych.getCurrentTrial && window.jsPsych.getCurrentTrial(); return !!(trial && trial.data && trial.data.condition === 'congruent'); })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { const trial = window.jsPsych && window.jsPsych.getCurrentTrial && window.jsPsych.getCurrentTrial(); return (trial && trial.data && trial.data.correct_response) || null; })()

stimuli.id: Incongruent Stroop stimulus (word mismatches ink color)

detection.selector: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus .stroop-stim'); if (!el) return false; const trial = window.jsPsych && window.jsPsych.getCurrentTrial && window.jsPsych.getCurrentTrial(); return !!(trial && trial.data && trial.data.condition === 'incongruent'); })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { const trial = window.jsPsych && window.jsPsych.getCurrentTrial && window.jsPsych.getCurrentTrial(); return (trial && trial.data && trial.data.correct_response) || null; })()

phase_detection.test: (() => { const trial = window.jsPsych && window.jsPsych.getCurrentTrial && window.jsPsych.getCurrentTrial(); return !!(trial && trial.data && trial.data.exp_stage === 'test'); })()
phase_detection.practice: (() => { const trial = window.jsPsych && window.jsPsych.getCurrentTrial && window.jsPsych.getCurrentTrial(); return !!(trial && trial.data && trial.data.exp_stage === 'practice'); })()
phase_detection.feedback: (() => { const trial = window.jsPsych && window.jsPsych.getCurrentTrial && window.jsPsych.getCurrentTrial(); const tid = trial && trial.data && trial.data.trial_id; return tid === 'instruction_feedback' || tid === 'practice_feedback' || tid === 'test_feedback'; })()

attention_check.response_js: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus'); const text = (el && el.textContent) ? el.textContent : ''; const m = text.match(/Press the (\w) key/i); if (m) return m[1].toLowerCase(); if (/third to last letter/i.test(text)) return 'x'; if (/second to last letter/i.test(text)) return 'y'; if (/the last letter/i.test(text)) return 'z'; if (/first letter/i.test(text)) return 'a'; if (/second letter/i.test(text)) return 'b'; if (/third letter/i.test(text)) return 'c'; return null; })()

task_specific.notes: Response keys (',', '.', '/') map to ink colors (red/blue/green) via one of 5 counterbalanced assignments selected by window.efVars.group_index. Correct key is read at runtime from jsPsych.getCurrentTrial().data.correct_response.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: ex-Gaussian mu values for the congruent (color-naming) condition were in the range of approximately 520-580 ms

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: younger adults' congruent ex-Gaussian mu was approximately 535 ms (vocal response)

citations.authors: Pratte, M. S., Rouder, J. N., Morey, R. D., & Feng, C.
citations.title: Exploring the differences in distributional properties between Stroop and Simon effects using delta plots
citations.quote: manual-key Stroop congruent mu around 580-640 ms

citations.authors: Eisenberg, I. W., Bissett, P. G., Enkavi, A. Z., Li, J., MacKinnon, D. P., Marsch, L. A., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Uncovering the structure of self-regulation through data-driven ontology discovery
citations.quote: manual 3-key Stroop congruent mean RT ~700 ms in MTurk adult sample

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: sigma estimates for color-naming congruent condition ~ 30-50 ms

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: young-adult congruent sigma estimates ~ 45-60 ms

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süss, H. M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: ex-Gaussian sigma in choice-RT tasks typically 40-70 ms with substantial individual variation

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: tau values for the congruent condition ranged from approximately 80-150 ms

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: young-adult congruent tau estimated near 80 ms; older adults considerably larger

citations.authors: Hervey, A. S., Epstein, J. N., Curry, J. F., et al.
citations.title: Reaction time distribution analysis of neuropsychological performance in an ADHD sample
citations.quote: tau parameter in choice-RT tasks for control children/adolescents ~ 75-130 ms

congruent.rationale: Manual 3-key Stroop with congruent trials yields mean RT ~700-720 ms in healthy adults. Ex-Gaussian decomposition (mu+tau = mean) places mu around 620 ms reflecting the modal Gaussian component for fast, conflict-free color identification. Sigma ~55 ms captures moderate Gaussian width typical of perceptual-motor RT. Tau ~90 ms reflects a modest right tail from occasional attentional lapses; manual responses produce smaller tau than vocal Stroop because articulation variability is removed.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: the typical incongruent-minus-congruent Stroop interference effect is on the order of 100 ms in manual response variants and larger in vocal versions

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: incongruent mu ~ 540-600 ms; the increase relative to congruent appeared mostly in tau, not mu

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: incongruent mu in young adults ~ 555 ms (vocal); manual variants run higher

citations.authors: Pratte, M. S., Rouder, J. N., Morey, R. D., & Feng, C.
citations.title: Exploring the differences in distributional properties between Stroop and Simon effects using delta plots
citations.quote: manual-key Stroop incongruent mu approximately 30-60 ms above congruent

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: sigma in incongruent condition modestly larger than congruent (~40-60 ms)

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: incongruent sigma 50-70 ms in young adults

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: the Stroop interference effect was reflected almost exclusively in increases in tau, not mu

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: incongruent tau in young adults ~ 110-140 ms vs ~80 ms congruent

citations.authors: Pratte, M. S., Rouder, J. N., Morey, R. D., & Feng, C.
citations.title: Exploring the differences in distributional properties between Stroop and Simon effects using delta plots
citations.quote: the Stroop effect grows with RT bin (positive delta slope), consistent with a tau increase

incongruent.rationale: Incongruent trials produce a Stroop interference effect of ~70-100 ms, with mean RT ~780-810 ms in manual key-response Stroop variants. The interference manifests primarily as an increase in tau (the exponential tail) reflecting episodes of conflict-driven response selection delay, with a smaller shift in mu. Sigma is slightly elevated relative to congruent reflecting greater within-condition variability in resolving lexical-color conflict.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: RT decreases as a power function of practice; early-block reductions are largest and successive blocks yield diminishing returns

citations.authors: Stroop, J. R.
citations.title: Studies of interference in serial verbal reactions
citations.quote: practice with the color-word task produced a substantial decrease in time required across early sessions

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Stroop interference attenuates only modestly with practice; overall RTs decline at a few percent per block early on

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: power-law fits show RT approaches asymptote within a small number of blocks for simple choice tasks

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Stroop performance stabilizes after a few blocks of practice in healthy adults

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Stroop accuracy in young adults is typically near ceiling (>90%) so accuracy gains across blocks are small

citations.authors: Eisenberg, I. W., Bissett, P. G., Enkavi, A. Z., Li, J., MacKinnon, D. P., Marsch, L. A., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Uncovering the structure of self-regulation through data-driven ontology discovery
citations.quote: Stroop accuracy was high (>90%) and stable across blocks in MTurk adult sample

practice_effect.rationale: Stroop RT decreases monotonically across early blocks as S-R mappings for the three color keys are consolidated. With only 4 practice trials/block and 3 test blocks of 40 trials, expect ~2-3% RT reduction per block early on, asymptoting after roughly 4 blocks of practice. Accuracy gains are small because ceiling is high from the start.

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M. A.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: responses immediately following errors were significantly slower than those following correct responses

citations.authors: Botvinick, M. M., Braver, T. S., Barch, D. M., Carter, C. S., & Cohen, J. D.
citations.title: Conflict monitoring and cognitive control
citations.quote: post-error slowing on the order of tens of milliseconds is reliably observed across conflict tasks

citations.authors: Notebaert, W., Houtman, F., Van Opstal, F., Gevers, W., Fias, W., & Verguts, T.
citations.title: Post-error slowing: An orienting account
citations.quote: post-error slowing magnitude in flanker/Stroop-style tasks typically 20-50 ms

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., Vandekerckhove, J., Forstmann, B. U., Keuleers, E., Brysbaert, M., & Wagenmakers, E. J.
citations.title: Testing theories of post-error slowing
citations.quote: post-error slowing of approximately 25-40 ms observed in lexical-decision and choice-RT data

citations.authors: Notebaert, W., Houtman, F., Van Opstal, F., Gevers, W., Fias, W., & Verguts, T.
citations.title: Post-error slowing: An orienting account
citations.quote: the slowing effect was largest on the trial immediately following the error and dissipated by the next trial

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., Vandekerckhove, J., Forstmann, B. U., Keuleers, E., Brysbaert, M., & Wagenmakers, E. J.
citations.title: Testing theories of post-error slowing
citations.quote: post-error slowing was largely confined to the trial immediately following the error

post_error_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing is reliably observed in Stroop and other conflict tasks (Rabbitt-style adjustments). Magnitude in healthy adults is typically 20-50 ms on the trial immediately following an error, decaying by the next trial. Reflects strategic control adjustment after conflict-related errors.

citations.authors: Gratton, G., Coles, M. G. H., & Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: the congruency effect on a given trial was reduced when the previous trial was incongruent compared with congruent

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: iI trials are typically 20-40 ms faster than cI trials in Stroop, indexing conflict-driven control adjustments

citations.authors: Kerns, J. G., et al.
citations.title: Anterior cingulate and prefrontal cortex: Strategic conflict adjustments
citations.quote: Stroop incongruent trials following incongruent showed reduced interference relative to those following congruent (~20-30 ms)

citations.authors: Gratton, G., Coles, M. G. H., & Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: congruent trials following incongruent showed slightly elevated RT relative to congruent following congruent

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: iC trials are typically only marginally slower than cC trials (effects of a few ms)

conflict_adaptation.rationale: Gratton/conflict-adaptation effect: incongruent trials following an incongruent trial (iI) show reduced interference (~20-30 ms faster than incongruent following congruent, cI). Congruent-after-incongruent (iC) shows minimal change vs congruent-after-congruent (cC). Documented in standard Stroop with manual responses; trial sequences in this task are randomized so the effect should emerge on average.

vigilance_decrement.rationale: Total test duration (~120 trials, ~4-5 minutes) is too short to elicit a reliable vigilance decrement in healthy adults. Stroop is also a high-engagement task that is relatively resistant to time-on-task slowing within a single short session.

time_on_task_drift.rationale: Short test session with attention-check breaks between blocks; published Stroop studies of this length do not reliably document monotonic RT drift beyond the practice/learning trajectory already captured by practice_effect.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: rt_mean_sd_ms ~90 ms reflects typical between-subject SD of overall mean RT in adult Stroop samples (mean RTs commonly span ~600-900 ms across individuals). rt_condition_sd_ms ~35 ms captures individual differences in the Stroop interference effect (effect ranges from ~40-150 ms across healthy adults). sigma_tau_range [0.7, 1.3] allows ex-Gaussian shape parameters to scale ±30% per subject, accommodating well-documented individual differences in tau (lapse-prone vs steady responders). accuracy_sd 0.03 reflects mild between-subject variability under high-accuracy ceiling. omission_sd 0.008 reflects small variability in deadline-miss rates given the 1500 ms response window.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süss, H. M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: between-subject SD of mean RT in choice-RT tasks ranged from approximately 70-110 ms in healthy adult samples

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Stroop interference effects across healthy adult studies range from approximately 40 to 150 ms

citations.authors: Eisenberg, I. W., Bissett, P. G., Enkavi, A. Z., Li, J., MacKinnon, D. P., Marsch, L. A., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Uncovering the structure of self-regulation through data-driven ontology discovery

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süss, H. M., & Wittmann, W. W.

citations.quote: individual estimates of tau in choice-RT tasks varied by roughly a factor of 2 across the sample

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: tau showed substantially greater inter-subject variability than mu in young adults

citations.title: Reaction time distribution analysis of neuropsychological performance in an ADHD sample
citations.quote: tau values in control children/adolescents spanned roughly 0.6-1.5x the group mean

citations.authors: Eisenberg, I. W., Bissett, P. G., Enkavi, A. Z., Li, J., MacKinnon, D. P., Marsch, L. A., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Uncovering the structure of self-regulation through data-driven ontology discovery
citations.quote: between-subject SD of Stroop accuracy in healthy adult sample was approximately 0.025-0.04

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review

citations.authors: Eisenberg, I. W., Bissett, P. G., Enkavi, A. Z., Li, J., MacKinnon, D. P., Marsch, L. A., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Uncovering the structure of self-regulation through data-driven ontology discovery

citations.authors: Hervey, A. S., Epstein, J. N., Curry, J. F., et al.
citations.title: Reaction time distribution analysis of neuropsychological performance in an ADHD sample

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'stroop_rdoc' with 3 stimuli. Source files: jquery.min.js, bootstrap.min.js, math.min.js, jspsych.js, plugin-instructions.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-fullscreen.js, plugin-survey-text.js, plugin-attention-check.js, experiment.j.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 3 temporal effects.
Produced 49 citations across 18 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 23 parameters; 5 are high-sensitivity.
